message.content:
You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Deflection](deflection.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[deflection]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path, the word count, and the FENs you used.

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: The X-ray (a long-range piece acts through an enemy or friendly piece on a line)
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: X-ray attack and X-ray defense: a rook, queen or bishop defends or attacks a square THROUGH an intervening piece. Distinguish from skewer.
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): C:/GrandMaster/03-Tactics/x-ray.md

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

input.content:
# X-ray

> An X-ray is when a long-range piece (rook, queen, or bishop) attacks or defends a square *through* an intervening enemy or friendly piece on the same line.

**Rating focus:** 1500–2000
**Prerequisites:** [Pins](pins.md), [Skewers](skewers.md), none required beyond basic tactics

## The idea

A rook, queen, or bishop "sees" along a rank, file, or diagonal. Normally a piece standing in the way blocks that vision. The **X-ray** exploits the fact that this blocker is temporary: either it can be removed, or — more subtly — the line-piece *still influences* the square behind the blocker even while the blocker stands there.

There are two distinct flavours:

- **X-ray attack:** your piece looks through an *enemy* piece to a valuable target behind it. When the front piece moves or is captured, your fire lands on the rear target.
- **X-ray defense:** your piece guards a friendly piece (or square) *through* an enemy piece that sits between them. The defender is "connected" to its protégé along the line even though an opponent piece is in between.

This is **not** a skewer. In a skewer, you attack two pieces on a line *right now* and force the front one to move so you win the back one. In an X-ray, the relationship works *through* the intermediate piece — most often the point is that a capture sequence resolves in your favour because your piece defends or attacks along the line after exchanges.

## Why it matters

X-ray motifs turn "impossible-looking" defenses into resources and "safe-looking" enemy pieces into targets. The classic practical payoff: you appear to be losing material on a square, but your rook or bishop is secretly defending it through the enemy piece — so the capture is safe. Or you sacrifice into an exchange that looks even, but your back piece re-establishes contact and wins. Recognizing the line through the blocker is what separates a player who counts captures one move deep from one who counts the *whole sequence*.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . P P
1 R . . r . . . K
```
*White's Ra1 defends along the back rank: it eyes d1 through Black's rook.*
**FEN:** 7k/6pp/8/8/8/8/6PP/R2r3K w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
[Analyze](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/6pp/8/8/8/8/6PP/R2r3K_w_-_-_0_1)

Black's rook on d1 looks like it wins the pawn-up endgame by activity, but White is fine: after **1.Rxd1** the rooks come off, because Ra1 X-rays through d1. More importantly, this geometry is the engine of the worked example below: a rook on an open file defends or attacks squares behind the first piece it meets.

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . . p .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . P .
1 . . . . R r q K
```
*X-ray defense: White's Re1 guards f1 through Black's rook on f1.*
**FEN:** 6k1/6p1/8/8/8/8/6P1/4Rrq1K w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
[Analyze](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/6p1/8/8/8/8/6P1/4Rrq1K_w_-_-_0_1)

Black threatens ...Qxf1#? No: White's Re1 X-rays the f1 square *through* the black rook. After **1.Rxf1 Qxf1+ 2.Kxf1**(? — actually the king cannot, so) the simple point is that f1 is defended by the rook along the rank, so any black piece landing on f1 can be recaptured. The rook on e1 protects f1 even though a black rook sits on it.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **X-ray attack (through enemy piece):** Bishop or rook bears on a target behind an enemy unit; removing/capturing the front unit exposes the rear one.
- **X-ray defense (through enemy piece):** Your rook/queen/bishop protects a friendly piece or key square along the line, with an enemy piece in between — so a "winning" capture is actually met by recapture.
- **X-ray through a friendly piece:** Doubled rooks or a queen-behind-rook battery; the rear piece supports the front one through it.
- **Self-X-ray / Alekhine's gun:** Q + R + R stacked on a file, each defending the one ahead.
- **Distinguish from skewer:** skewer = front piece must move *now*, you grab the back piece. X-ray = the line relationship survives the exchange.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Long range piece on open line] --> B{Enemy or friendly piece between it and a key square}
  B -->|yes| C{Is the key square a target or my own piece}
  B -->|no| Z[No xray just a normal threat]
  C -->|enemy target behind| D[Xray attack remove the front piece]
  C -->|my piece or square behind| E[Xray defense capture is safe]
  D --> F[Calculate the capture sequence to the end]
  E --> F
  F --> G[Confirm my piece re-establishes contact]
```

## Worked examples

**Example 1 — X-ray defense saves a rook.**

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . P P
1 . . R r . . . K
```
*White to move; the c1 rook X-rays d1 through Black's rook.*
**FEN:** 6k1/6pp/8/8/8/8/6PP/2Rr3K w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
[Analyze](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/6pp/8/8/8/8/6PP/2Rr3K_w_-_-_0_1)

Solution: **1.Rxd1** wins the black rook for free. Black's Rd1 sat on a square that White's Rc1 controlled *through* it — the moment Black placed the rook there, it was an X-ray target along the first rank.

**Example 2 — X-ray attack with the queen.**

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . r . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . P P
1 . . . . . Q . K
```
*White to move; the f1 queen looks through Rf7 to f8/the king's shelter.*
**FEN:** 6k1/5r2/8/8/8/8/6PP/5Q1K w - - 0 1
Side to move: **White**
[Analyze](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/5r2/8/8/8/8/6PP/5Q1K_w_-_-_0_1)

Solution: White's queen and the black rook share the f-file. **1.Qf5** keeps the pressure, but the clean point is the X-ray: the queen attacks f7 *and* eyes the king's hiding squares behind it. If the rook ever leaves the f-file, **Qf8+** or **Qxf7** becomes available — the front rook is pinned-by-purpose to the file. Black must keep defending along f, restricting his counterplay while White improves with the king and pawns.

## Common mistakes

- **Confusing X-ray with skewer.** Ask: does the front piece *have* to move now (skewer), or does my line-relationship survive the trade (X-ray)?
- **Counting captures only one ply deep.** The whole point lives at the *end* of the sequence — calculate the full exchange.
- **Forgetting the blocker can be defended.** An X-ray defense fails if the recapturing piece is itself pinned or removable.
- **Assuming the rear target is loose.** Verify the square behind the blocker is actually weak or valuable before committing.
- **Overlooking your *own* X-ray defenses** when you think you're losing material.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle themes:** train [X-ray attack](https://lichess.org/training/xRayAttack) — do 20 puzzles, then revisit missed ones the next day. Mix in `pin`, `skewer`, and `defensiveMove` themes (15 each) to sharpen the distinction.
- **Books:** *Chess Tactics from Scratch* (Weteschnik) — the chapter on "geometrical motifs" treats X-rays rigorously. *The Woodpecker Method* (Smith/Tikkanen) for spaced-repetition reps.
- **Drill:** set up the four diagrams above from memory and verbalize the line through the blocker, 3 reps each across the week.
- **Endgame link:** play out Lucena/Philidor rook endings — rook X-ray defenses appear constantly.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `6k1/6pp/8/8/8/8/6PP/2Rr3K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win material.
2. **FEN:** `7k/6pp/8/8/8/8/6PP/R2r3K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Is taking on d1 safe, and why?
3. **FEN:** `6k1/5r2/8/8/8/8/6PP/5Q1K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Identify the X-ray relationship and Black's resulting obligation.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Rxd1** — Rc1 X-rayed d1 through the black rook, winning a whole rook.
2. **Yes, 1.Rxd1 is safe.** Ra1 controls d1 through Black's rook (X-ray along rank 1), so the trade simply liquidates into an equal/holdable endgame — Black has no in-between resource.
3. The white queen and black rook share the **f-file**; the queen X-rays f7 and the squares behind it toward the king. Black is obliged to **keep the rook on the f-file** (or otherwise guard f7/f8), restricting his activity, since any departure invites **Qxf7** or a check on the f-file.

</details>

## Related

- [Pins](pins.md) — front piece cannot move; contrast with X-ray.
- [Skewers](skewers.md) — the "reverse pin"; closest cousin to the X-ray.
- [Deflection](deflection.md) — often the mechanism that removes the X-ray blocker.
- [Batteries](batteries.md) — friendly-piece X-rays (doubled rooks, queen behind rook).

Tags: [[x-ray]] [[skewer]] [[pin]] [[deflection]] [[battery]]
